JavaScript Hoisting: Why Does That Variable Say undefined?
I was sitting in on a JavaScript Q&A session today, and somewhere in the middle of it I realized my mental model of this concept was a little off. So I'm writing it down to set the record straight.
JavaScript has this peculiar thing called hoisting — sometimes translated as "lifting" or "raising up."
MDN has a decent explanation of it:

Hoisting looks like it simply moves variable and function declarations to the top of the program block, but that's not quite what happens. Variable and function declarations are put into memory during the compile phase, but their actual position in the code stays exactly where you wrote them.
So from that you can tell hoisting is really about memory. Before JavaScript runs, it lifts variable declarations (
) up front and stores them in memory, then later does the assignments or other work in the right spots.

If we apply the idea from above, let's break the problem apart and see what's really going on:

This is another pretty common hoisting example. Normally code runs top to bottom, but because of hoisting the

) inside the function, though, only get created when you actually run the function — and once the function finishes, those variables get destroyed. So work done inside a function tends to be a bit easier on memory.
So basically, hoisting lifts variables and functions to the very top of your code and stashes them in memory first, then does the assignments and the rest afterward.
From that you can see hoisting is mainly about memory — and at the end of the day, hoisting is just a concept, not some magical code rearrangement.
There are two key things about the JavaScript execution environment.
When it runs, it loads these two things:
the creation phase, which also sets up the outer environment
The creation phase is where variables and functions get created first, and then the execution phase handles the assignments and everything else.

During a JavaScript Q&A session I realized my mental model of hoisting was a little off, so I wrote this down to set it straight. This post walks through what hoisting actually is, why a variable can log undefined before it's assigned, how function declarations get hoisted, and how it all ties back to memory.
